## Definition

**Phospholipid** is introduced in this chapter. It represents a double layer of fat that contains phospholipid molecules. Phospholipids are lipids with a phosphate group attached, and they are one of the main classes of membrane lipids alongside cholesterol (a steroid with a hydroxyl group attached) and glycolipids (lipids with carbohydrate groups attached).

## Core Principles

Classifier signals: freq_multi_section.

Phospholipids form the basic structural framework of biological membranes — the *lipid bilayer*. The membrane itself consists of lipids and proteins (to which carbohydrates may be attached), with the lipid bilayer providing the foundational architecture into which membrane proteins are embedded.

Plasma membrane proteins are divided into two categories — *integral proteins* and *peripheral proteins* — both of which interact with the phospholipid bilayer to support membrane function.

## Sub-concepts

- **Lipid bilayer**: the double-layered phospholipid sheet forming the basic structural framework of the plasma membrane.
- **Other membrane lipids**: cholesterol (steroid with a hydroxyl group) and glycolipids (lipids with carbohydrate groups attached) coexist with phospholipids in the membrane.
- **Membrane proteins** associated with the phospholipid bilayer:
  - *Integral proteins* — most are *transmembrane proteins*, spanning the lipid bilayer and contacting both intracellular and extracellular fluid.
  - *Peripheral proteins* — associate with membrane lipids or integral proteins at either the inner or outer surface of the membrane.

## Field-level Controversies

## 文獻分歧 / Discussion

## Practical Applications

Membrane proteins anchored within or onto the phospholipid bilayer carry out a variety of functions essential for maintaining cell viability, illustrating the functional role of the phospholipid bilayer as a platform for protein-mediated cellular processes.
